seo.imageAlt: A bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with chicken, garnished with sesame seeds and cilantro.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a culinary masterpiece from Oaxaca, Mexico.

description: Mole Negro, the queen of Oaxacan moles, is a majestic dish boasting an incredible depth of flavor from a complex blend of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and chocolate. This rich, dark sauce offers a symphony of sweet, savory, and smoky notes that is truly unforgettable. Perfect for special occasions or a luxurious weeknight dinner.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the dried chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 30 seconds per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Remove from heat, place in a bowl, and cover with hot water. Let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft, then drain, reserving some soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Roast Vegetables and Fry Aromatics
instructions.text: On a comal or baking sheet, roast the tomatillos, tomatoes, garlic, and onion until softened and slightly charred. Meanwhile, in a separate skillet with 1 tablespoon of lard or oil, fry the plantain slices until golden brown. Remove and set aside. In the same skillet, briefly toast the almonds, pecans, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, raisins, cinnamon stick, cloves, and peppercorns until fragrant, about 2-3 minutes. Set aside.

instructions.title: Blend the First Paste
instructions.text: In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, roasted tomatillos, tomatoes, garlic, onion, and a small amount of the reserved chile soaking liquid or chicken broth. Blend until a smooth paste forms, adding more liquid as needed. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl, pressing firmly to extract all liquid, discarding any solids. This step is crucial for a smooth mole.

instructions.title: Blend the Second Paste
instructions.text: In the same blender, combine the fried plantain, toasted nuts, seeds, raisins, cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, dried oregano, and thyme with about 1/2 cup of chicken broth. Blend until very smooth. You may need to do this in batches. This mixture will be thick.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Base
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of lard or oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the strained chile paste and cook, stirring constantly, for about 10-15 minutes until it darkens and thickens significantly. This step is essential for developing the deep flavors of the mole. Add the second blended paste to the pot and continue to cook for another 5 minutes, stirring well to combine.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season the Mole
instructions.text: Gradually stir in the remaining chicken broth, a little at a time, until the mole reaches your desired consistency. It should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Add the Mexican chocolate and stir until it completely melts and is incorporated. Season with salt to taste and a pinch of sugar if needed to balance the flavors. Simmer gently for at least 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, allowing the flavors to meld. Serve hot with cooked chicken pieces.

tips: Always toast your chiles until fragrant, but never burn them, as burned chiles will make your mole bitter. A quick toast is all you need.
For an extra rich and dark mole, consider adding a small piece of a toasted tortilla or a bolillo (Mexican bread) to the second blend of ingredients.

Mole Negro can be made ahead of time. In fact, it often tastes even better the next day after the flavors have had more time to meld. Store it in the refrigerator for up to 5 days or freeze for up to 3 months.
article:
Embarking on the culinary journey of Oaxacan Black Mole, or Mole Negro, is to delve into the heart of Mexican gastronomy. This isn't just a sauce; it's a symphony of flavors, a testament to centuries of culinary tradition, and a dish revered as the "king" of Oaxacan moles. For those seeking an authentic taste of Mexico, mastering this complex yet rewarding creation is an unparalleled experience. It’s more than just a meal; it’s a celebration of culture, history, and an incredible array of ingredients that come together to create something truly magical.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro)?

Mole, derived from the Nahuatl word "mōlli" meaning "sauce," is a generic term for a variety of rich sauces in Mexican cuisine. Among its many iterations, Oaxacan Black Mole stands out for its profound depth of flavor, dark color, and intricate preparation. Often served during special occasions, holidays, and family gatherings, Mole Negro is a culinary masterpiece known for its balance of smoky, sweet, spicy, and earthy notes.

The sauce's signature darkness comes from a meticulous process of charring and toasting a specific blend of chiles—primarily chilhuacle negro, mulato, pasilla mexicano, and ancho. These are complemented by an extensive list of ingredients including nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, vegetables, and, famously, Mexican chocolate. The result is a thick, glossy, and intensely flavorful sauce that coats the palate with layers of complexity, making it an unforgettable part of any Mexican dinner recipes repertoire.

## Ingredients You Need for Oaxacan Black Mole

Creating an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe requires a commitment to sourcing quality ingredients. While the list may seem daunting, each component plays a crucial role in building the mole's distinctive flavor profile.

For an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe, you will need:

*   **Dried Chiles:**
    *   6-8 chilhuacle negro chiles (essential for color and flavor, if unavailable, increase mulato)
    *   4-5 mulato chiles
    *   4-5 pasilla mexicano chiles
    *   3-4 ancho chiles
*   **Aromatics & Vegetables:**
    *   1 large white onion, roughly chopped
    *   6-8 cloves garlic
    *   2 ripe tomatoes, quartered
    *   2 tomatillos, husked and quartered
    *   1 plantain, peeled and sliced
    *   1/4 cup raisins
    *   1/4 cup pitted prunes
*   **Nuts & Seeds:**
    *   1/4 cup almonds
    *   1/4 cup peanuts
    *   2 tablespoons sesame seeds
    *   1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
*   **Spices:**
    *   1 tablespoon whole black peppercorns
    *   1 teaspoon whole cloves
    *   1 cinnamon stick (about 2-3 inches)
    *   1 teaspoon cumin seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon coriander seeds
    *   Pinch of dried thyme
    *   Pinch of dried oregano
    *   1-2 bay leaves
*   **Thickeners & Sweeteners:**
    *   2-3 stale corn tortillas or 1 slice of day-old bread, torn
    *   1/4 cup Mexican chocolate (Ibarra or Abuelita), roughly chopped
    *   1-2 tablespoons piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar, to taste
*   **Liquids & Fats:**
    *   6-8 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian option)
    *   1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil
    *   Salt, to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Mole Negro

Making an Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe is a labor of love, but the process, while lengthy, is methodical.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Destem and deseed all dried chiles. Open them flat. In a dry skillet or comal over medium heat, toast the chiles in batches until fragrant and slightly puffed, being careful not to burn them (this develops bitterness). Alternatively, some recipes call for briefly frying them in hot oil. Once toasted, soak the chiles in very hot water for 20-30 minutes until softened. Drain, reserving some soaking liquid.
2.  **Toast Nuts and Seeds:** In a clean dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until golden and fragrant. Set aside.
3.  **Fry Aromatics and Spices:** Heat lard or oil in a large pot or skillet. Fry the onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomatillos, plantain, raisins, and prunes until softened and lightly browned. Add the whole peppercorns, cloves, cinnamon stick, cumin, coriander, thyme, and oregano, frying for another minute until fragrant. Add the stale tortillas or bread and fry until crispy.
4.  **Blend Everything:** Transfer all fried ingredients, toasted nuts, seeds, and the softened chiles to a high-powered blender. Add 2-3 cups of chicken broth and blend until completely smooth. This may take several minutes and require adding more broth or reserved chile soaking liquid to achieve a very fine paste. For the smoothest mole, pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing firmly to extract all liquid and discarding any solids.
5.  **Cook the Mole:** Heat a fresh tablespoon of lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Carefully pour in the mole paste. It will splatter, so be cautious. Stir constantly for 10-15 minutes, allowing the paste to "fry" and darken further. This step is crucial for deepening the flavor.
6.  **Simmer and Season:** Gradually add the remaining chicken broth, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Add the bay leaves. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer, then reduce heat to low, cover, and cook for at least 1-2 hours, stirring occasionally. The longer it simmers, the more the flavors meld.
7.  **Finish the Mole:** After simmering, stir in the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar) until fully dissolved. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt. The mole should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. If it's too thick, add more broth; if too thin, simmer uncovered until it reaches the desired consistency.

## Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

*   **Quality Chiles are Key:** The success of your Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe hinges on the quality and authenticity of your dried chiles. Seek out a Mexican grocery store for the best selection, especially for chilhuacle negro.
*   **Don't Burn Anything:** When toasting chiles, nuts, and spices, low and slow is the mantra. Burning will introduce bitterness that can ruin the entire batch.
*   **Blend, Blend, Blend:** Patience is vital during the blending stage. The smoother the paste, the more luxurious your mole will be. Straining is highly recommended for a truly refined texture.
*   **The Fry is Essential:** The initial frying of the mole paste in the pot (often called "sazonar") is non-negotiable. It deepens the color and flavor dramatically.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is about balance. Continuously taste throughout the simmering process and adjust for sweetness, salt, and spice.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole Negro tastes even better the next day, allowing the complex flavors to fully meld. It also freezes beautifully.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

The traditional way to enjoy Oaxacan Black Mole is spooned generously over succulent pieces of chicken or turkey. However, its versatility extends far beyond poultry.

*   **Classic:** Serve with slow-cooked chicken or turkey, accompanied by Mexican red rice and warm corn tortillas.
*   **Enchiladas:** Use mole as the sauce for enchiladas, filled with shredded chicken or cheese.
*   **Tamales:** A fantastic filling for tamales, often with pork or chicken.
*   **Vegetarian:** Delicious over roasted sweet potatoes, grilled portobello mushrooms, or even with scrambled eggs.
*   **Other Proteins:** Try it with pork loin, duck breast, or even beef short ribs.
*   **Garnish:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, chopped fresh onion, or cilantro adds a beautiful touch and textural contrast.

## Variations and Substitutions for Oaxacan Black Mole

While the classic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe is a masterpiece, home cooks can adapt it based on availability and preference.

*   **Chiles:** If chilhuacle negro chiles are impossible to find, increase the amount of mulato and ancho chiles for a similar dark hue and smoky depth, though the flavor profile will subtly shift.
*   **Nuts & Seeds:** Feel free to vary the type of nuts and seeds (e.g., cashews, walnuts), maintaining a similar total quantity for thickening and flavor.
*   **Sweeteners:** Piloncillo is traditional, but dark brown sugar can be used. Adjust the amount based on your desired sweetness.
*   **Chocolate:** Good quality dark chocolate (70% cocoa or higher) can substitute Mexican chocolate, though you might need to add a touch more sugar as Mexican chocolate is pre-sweetened.
*   **Vegetarian Mole:** Simply use a robust vegetable broth instead of chicken broth. The richness of the other ingredients will ensure a flavorful sauce.
*   **Shortcut:** For those short on time, high-quality jarred mole paste can be a starting point. While not as complex as homemade, it offers a taste of this incredible sauce. Always "fry" the paste in oil before adding broth and personalizing with fresh spices or chocolate.

Making your own Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary adventure that connects you to the heart of Mexican tradition. It's an investment of time and effort that yields an incredibly rewarding and deeply satisfying dish, one that will undoubtedly become a cherished part of your Mexican dinner recipes collection.
